quiz_questions.question_text: What is a covalent bond?

options.text: A bond formed by transferring electrons
A bond formed by the sharing of electron pairs between atoms

quiz_questions.explanation: A bond formed by the sharing of electron pairs between atoms is correct. This is a key concept in Covalent Bonds.

quiz_questions.question_text: A single covalent bond consists of:

options.text: One shared pair of electrons (2 electrons)

quiz_questions.explanation: A single bond = 1 shared electron pair = 2 electrons shared between two atoms.

quiz_questions.question_text: A double covalent bond consists of:

options.text: Two shared pairs of electrons (4 electrons)

quiz_questions.explanation: Double bond = 2 shared pairs = 4 electrons. E.g., O=O in O₂; C=O in CO₂.

quiz_questions.question_text: A triple covalent bond consists of:

options.text: Three shared pairs of electrons (6 electrons)

quiz_questions.explanation: Triple bond = 3 shared pairs = 6 electrons. E.g., N≡N in N₂; C≡C in alkynes.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which molecule has a triple bond?
quiz_questions.explanation: N₂ has a triple bond (N≡N). Bond order = 3, very high bond energy (945 kJ/mol), making N₂ very stable and unreactive.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a Lewis structure, a pair of dots represents:
quiz_questions.explanation: Lone pairs (non-bonding pairs) are shown as two dots (::) on atoms in Lewis structures. They satisfy the octet but are not shared.

quiz_questions.question_text: The VSEPR model predicts:

options.text: Molecular geometry based on minimizing repulsion between electron pairs

quiz_questions.explanation: VSEPR (Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion): electron pairs around a central atom arrange to minimize repulsion, determining molecular shape.

quiz_questions.question_text: A molecule with 2 bonding pairs and no lone pairs around the central atom is:
quiz_questions.explanation: 2 bonding pairs, no lone pairs → linear (180°). Example: CO₂ (O=C=O, all bonding pairs around C, which has none).

quiz_questions.question_text: A water molecule (H₂O) is bent because:

options.text: The 2 lone pairs on oxygen repel the bonding pairs, bending the H-O-H angle to ~104.5°

quiz_questions.explanation: Water: O has 2 bonding pairs (to H) and 2 lone pairs. Lone pair-lone pair and lone pair-bond pair repulsion compresses the H-O-H angle to ~104.5° (bent).

quiz_questions.question_text: Polar covalent bonds form when:

options.text: The atoms have no electronegativity difference
Two atoms share electrons unequally due to electronegativity difference (0.5–1.7)

quiz_questions.explanation: Polar covalent: unequal electron sharing → δ+ on less electronegative atom, δ- on more electronegative atom. E.g., H-Cl, H-O.

quiz_questions.question_text: Nonpolar covalent bonds form when:

options.text: Two atoms share electrons equally (same or similar electronegativity, ΔEN < 0.5)

quiz_questions.explanation: Nonpolar covalent: equal sharing. Example: H-H, Cl-Cl, C-C. No partial charges.

quiz_questions.question_text: The bond in Cl₂ is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Cl-Cl: identical atoms → same electronegativity → equal electron sharing → nonpolar covalent bond.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is the correct Lewis structure for HCl?

options.text: H-Cl: with 3 lone pairs on Cl
H:Cl: with Cl having 3 lone pairs and H having 1 lone pair

quiz_questions.explanation: HCl: H shares 1 electron with Cl (single bond). Cl has 3 lone pairs. H has no lone pairs. Structure: H-Cl: (with 3 lone pairs on Cl).

quiz_questions.question_text: In a Lewis structure for CH₄:

options.text: Carbon forms 4 single bonds to hydrogen (no lone pairs on C)

quiz_questions.explanation: CH₄ (methane): C has 4 valence electrons, each H has 1. C forms 4 single bonds to H. C and each H achieve stable electron configuration. No lone pairs on C.

quiz_questions.question_text: Bond length:

options.text: Decreases as bond order increases (triple < double < single)
Is the same for all bonds

quiz_questions.explanation: Triple bonds are shorter and stronger than double bonds, which are shorter and stronger than single bonds. More shared electrons pull nuclei closer together.

quiz_questions.question_text: Bond energy:

options.text: Increases as bond order increases (triple > double > single)

quiz_questions.explanation: Higher bond order → stronger bond → more energy required to break. Bond energies: triple > double > single.

quiz_questions.question_text: A resonance structure occurs when:

options.text: A molecule can be drawn with multiple valid Lewis structures that differ in bond placement (electrons delocalized)
A molecule has two different shapes

quiz_questions.explanation: Resonance: when a single Lewis structure can't accurately represent a molecule, multiple structures are drawn (e.g., ozone O₃). The real structure is a hybrid (average) of all resonance forms.

quiz_questions.question_text: Ozone (O₃) has resonance because:

options.text: The molecule is symmetric with no bonds
The central O-O bonds are equivalent (between single and double bond character), not fixed

quiz_questions.explanation: O₃ has 2 resonance structures where the double bond alternates. The actual structure has equal bond lengths (~128 pm, between O=O 121 pm and O-O 148 pm).

quiz_questions.question_text: An expanded octet occurs when:

options.text: A central atom has more than 8 electrons due to d-orbital availability (e.g., PCl₅, SF₆)

quiz_questions.explanation: Expanded octets: elements in period 3+ can accommodate >8 electrons using d orbitals. PCl₅ (10 e around P), SF₆ (12 e around S).

quiz_questions.question_text: The formal charge on an atom in a Lewis structure is:

options.text: Valence electrons − (lone pair e⁻) − ½(bonding e⁻)

quiz_questions.explanation: Formal charge = (valence e⁻) − (lone pair e⁻) − ½(bonding e⁻). Used to determine best Lewis structure (minimize formal charges, negative on most electronegative atom).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which shape does a tetrahedral molecule (like CH₄) have?
quiz_questions.explanation: CH₄: 4 bonding pairs, no lone pairs → tetrahedral geometry, bond angles ~109.5°.

quiz_questions.question_text: NH₃ (ammonia) has a trigonal pyramidal shape because:

options.text: N has 3 bonding pairs and 1 lone pair; the lone pair pushes bonding pairs into a pyramid

quiz_questions.explanation: NH₃: N has 3 bonds to H + 1 lone pair. VSEPR: 4 electron pairs → tetrahedral electron geometry, but trigonal pyramidal molecular geometry (lone pair is not an atom). Bond angles ≈107°.

quiz_questions.question_text: Electronegativity of fluorine compared to other elements:

options.text: F has the highest electronegativity (3.98) of all elements

quiz_questions.explanation: Fluorine is the most electronegative element (Pauling scale: F = 3.98, O = 3.44, N = 3.04). It attracts bonding electrons most strongly.

quiz_questions.question_text: A coordinate covalent bond (dative bond) is:

options.text: A covalent bond where both electrons come from one atom (the Lewis base)
An ionic bond where both electrons come from the metal

quiz_questions.explanation: Coordinate/dative bond: one atom donates both electrons. Example: NH₃ + H⁺ → NH₄⁺ (N donates lone pair to H⁺); also O→metal bonds in coordination compounds.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sigma (σ) bonds are:

options.text: End-to-end orbital overlap; present in all single bonds and as first bond in multiple bonds

quiz_questions.explanation: σ bonds: direct (end-on) overlap of orbitals along the internuclear axis. All single bonds are σ bonds. Double bonds = 1σ + 1π; triple bonds = 1σ + 2π.

quiz_questions.question_text: Pi (π) bonds are:

options.text: Side-to-side (lateral) orbital overlap; present in double and triple bonds

quiz_questions.explanation: π bonds: lateral overlap of p orbitals above and below the internuclear axis. π bonds are weaker than σ bonds and are found in double (1π) and triple (2π) bonds.

quiz_questions.question_text: Hybridization of carbon in CH₄ is:
quiz_questions.explanation: CH₄: C has 4 equivalent single bonds → sp³ hybridization (1 s + 3 p orbitals mix to form 4 equivalent sp³ orbitals). Tetrahedral geometry, 109.5° angles.

quiz_questions.question_text: Hybridization of carbon in C₂H₄ (ethylene) is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Each C in C₂H₄: 1 double bond to other C + 2 single bonds to H. sp² hybridization (1s+2p → 3 sp² orbitals). The double bond = 1σ (sp²-sp²) + 1π (p-p side overlap).

quiz_questions.question_text: Hybridization of carbon in C₂H₂ (acetylene) is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Each C in C₂H₂: 1 triple bond + 1 single bond → sp hybridization. Linear geometry. Triple bond = 1σ + 2π.

quiz_questions.question_text: Molecular polarity depends on:

options.text: Both the polarity of individual bonds AND the molecular geometry (symmetry may cancel dipoles)

quiz_questions.explanation: A molecule is polar if it has polar bonds AND the dipoles don't cancel. E.g., CO₂ (nonpolar, symmetric) vs. H₂O (polar, bent). Shape determines if bond dipoles cancel.
